item.title: You can now score Code Vein for its lowest price ever on Steam, but only if you're quick
item.itunes:title: You can now score Code Vein for its lowest price ever on Steam, but only if you're quick
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Code Vein is now 85% off on Steam: A steep discount at $8.99.
  • Despite mixed reviews, the game straddles the gap between Souls-like and JRPG.
  • Check out reviews and guides before diving into Code Vein for a more enjoyable experience.

If you're on the hunt for something new to play, Steam usually has a good deal on a gaming going at any given time. For this week, if you're a fan of Dark Souls-like games but want something different, Code Vein is now on sale at a steep discount. And while it's not a perfect title, people may find the sale big enough to give this game a shot.

 Related
10 underrated apps every PC user should check out

Whether you're an avid gamer or a productivity-oriented user, there's something here for everyone

4

Code Vein is now 85% off on Steam

Over on Steam, Code Vein is now cheaper than ever at 85% off. If you check out Code Vein's SteamDB page, you'll see that this is the steepest discount the game will ever go for. This means you can pick up Code Vein for a very agreeable $8.99, so if you've been waiting on it for get more affordable, now's your chance. The deal goes away on June 11th, so act fast.

So, how does the game fare? Well, despite being a pretty popular Dark Souls-like title, it suffered quite a bit on the review front. At the time of writing, Steam's user reviews has it pinned at 84% lifetime positive reviews, which isn't bad, but not entirely inspiring either. Fortunately, if you're curious to see if the game will gel for you, you can now give it a go for a very affordable price.

If you want more thoughts on Code Vein before you put your money down for it, our wonderful sister website TheGamer wrote a review for Code Vein, giving it a respectful 3 stars out of 5:

Code Vein is an enjoyable game with its own fair share of flaws. Mainly, it would benefit massively from more comprehensive player guidance and improving upon its atrocious female character design. Still, it manages to be a fun and colorful game that straddles the gap between a Souls-like game with a JRPG flair and a JRPG with a Souls-like influence.

If that doesn't sate you, be sure to also check out the five best (and worst) things about Code Vein, plus the five things it does better (and worse) than Dark Souls. And if you do decide to take the plunge, you can make the experience more enjoyable by checking out these ten things you should know when starting out with Code Vein.

Your Rating

Rate Now
0/10

Your comment has not been saved

item.title: Snag this beastly mini PC with an AMD Ryzen 5 and 32GB RAM at its lowest price ever
item.itunes:title: Snag this beastly mini PC with an AMD Ryzen 5 and 32GB RAM at its lowest price ever
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
KAMRUI Mini PC Computer
$280 $400 Save $120

A powerful mini PC that delivers plenty of power thanks to its powerful AMD Ryzen 5 processor and 32GB of RAM. Get it now for its best price as it drops to just $270.

$280 at Amazon

We've seen our share of mini PCs that go on sale. But if you're looking for one that can go above and beyond when it comes to performance, but at the same time won't break the bank, this one from Kamrui is going to be for you. While it's normally priced at $400, it can now be had for its best price thanks to a rare $120 discount that drops it down to just $280.

Related
5 things my mini PC does better than my mid-tower computer

The advantages swell beyond physical size

6

Not only is it compact and sleek, but you also get plenty of performance on the inside as well. This particular model features an AMD Ryzen 5 processor that's paired with 32GB of DDR4 RAM and 512GB of SSD storage. So, whether it's for work, or you need something for home and entertainment purposes, we don't think you'll be disappointed here.

What's great about the Kamrui mini PC?

Source: Kamrui

This is one of those devices you need to pick up if you've been looking to downsize your desktop PC setup without breaking the bank. Kamrui knows what it's doing when it comes to building mini PCs, and this one delivers tons of power thanks to its Ryzen 5 7430U CPU that can crank up to 4.3Ghz when needed.

It's also extremely efficient when it comes to power draw, with a default TDP of 15W, and a maximum of 28W. In addition to the above, the PC also comes with 32GB of DDR4 RAM, which can be expanded to 64GB if needed. There's also 512GB of SSD storage, which can also be expanded as well, with support for a maximum of 2TB.

The PC also has AMD Radeon RX Vega 7 graphics, which can handle most simple workloads, and also play games as well. Of course, you'll want to temper expectations since this isn't going to be the ideal setup for gaming. With that said, you'll be able to output to three monitors, with support for up to 4K at 60Hz.

As far as connectivity goes, you get an HDMI port, USB-C, DisplayPort, Ethernet, USB-A, and a 3.5mm audio jack. The PC also features wireless support for Wi-Fi 6 and Bluetooth 5.2. Overall, not a bad set of specifications for just $280. Just make sure to clip the coupon before checkout to save $120.

item.title: You are using Excel wrong if you haven’t tried its Python integration
item.itunes:title: You are using Excel wrong if you haven’t tried its Python integration
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Excel has been the undisputed king of spreadsheets for decades. Countless professionals rely on it daily for everything from simple data tracking to complex financial modelling. But if I told you that despite its countless capabilities, you might actually be underutilizing or even using it wrong. It involves blending Excel’s user-friendly interface with the analytical horsepower of Python.

If you haven’t explored this powerful integration, you are missing out on a world of efficiency, automation, and advanced data manipulation that can transform your entire workflow.

 Related
VBA vs. Python: Which is the best tool for Excel automation

Understanding the future of Excel automation

5

Getting started is easier than you think

No need to install Python separately

When I refer to Python, I don’t mean to use a dedicated Python app in favor of Excel. Of course, it comes with several advantages, but nothing beats a direct Python integration right into a familiar Excel canvas. The beauty of this integration is that you don’t abandon your familiar workshop (Excel). You still interact with your data in Excel’s intuitive grid, but the complex processing, automation, and advanced analysis happen behind the scenes, powered by Python.

Suppose you are a sales analyst and you have a large Excel sheet with customer transaction data, including columns like Customer ID, Product Category, Sales Amount, Region, and Transaction Date. Now you want to quickly identify the top three product categories by sales amount within each region for the last quarter, to prioritize marketing efforts.

With a traditional Excel method, you need to filter dates, set up pivot tables, and further filter that data. It also doesn’t easily show the actual rank within the pivot table itself without extra steps.

Now, with Python in Excel, you can write a Python expression directly in a cell to achieve this. You can go to a new cell and type =PY() to enter Python code. Inside the =PY() block, you can write Python code that leverages pandas to process your Excel data. Here’s what happens afterwards.

  • Excel takes your sales data table and converts it into a pandas DataFrame in the cloud.
  • The Python code executes in the cloud environment.
  • The pandas operations filter the data, group it by region, sort the product category by sales amount within each region, and then select the top three for each.
  • It displays the resulting DataFrame directly into your Excel sheet.

You can read my dedicated post to learn to perform advanced data analysis using Python in Excel.

 Related
How you can combine Python with Excel to supercharge your spreadsheets

Transform your Excel workflows with Python magic

6

A robust support for Python libraries

Powered by Anaconda

Source: Microsoft

When I first heard about Python in Excel, my immediate thought was, ‘How much real Python power does it bring?’ Going through the official documentation, I quickly discovered that Microsoft’s attempt wasn’t half-hearted. They have truly opened the gates to a massive, robust world of open-source libraries.

When you type =PY() into a cell, you are not just getting a bare-bones Python interpreter. Microsoft has integrated a set of the most widely used and powerful Python libraries directly into the Excel environment.

For example, Pandas can handle data cleaning, data transformation like pivoting, melting, joining, merging different tables within a workbook, complex aggregations, and more. It’s like having a super-powered VLOOKUP and SUMIFS on steroids that can handle hundreds of rows efficiently. It’s one of the Excel tricks that can save you hours every week.

Let’s move to another library. The built-in Excel chart types and customization options always left me wanting more. With Matplotlib and Seaborn available, I can now generate professional, highly customizable charts directly with my Excel data. Whether I need a scatter plot with custom labels, a correlation heatmap, or a distribution plot, I can generate them using Python code.

Overall, you don’t need to worry about installing Python, setting up environments, or managing dependencies.

Python is the future of spreadsheet productivity

Who needs complex formulas, anyway?

I have often hit the wall with Excel when dealing with truly large datasets. Python, with libraries like Pandas, simply laughs at those limitations. I can process and analyze massive amounts of data efficiently, all within the Excel environment I know and love. I don’t need to export to external tools just because the dataset got too big.

Besides, I’m no longer limited to functions like VLOOKUP and SUMIFS for analysis. I’m just a =PY() formula away from performing advanced statistical tests, building predictive models, and identifying complex patterns.

Data analysis reimagined

Overall, if you have been relying solely on traditional Excel methods, you are leaving potential on the table. By adopting Python integration, you visualize insights in ways that Excel alone simply can’t match.

Of course, it comes with a learning curve, especially if you haven’t used Python before. However, there is ample documentation and tutorials out there to get yourself familiar with specific Python libraries. If you often deal with complex datasets in Excel and struggle to get meaningful insights from them, I highly recommend giving it a shot.

item.title: 5 Assassin's Creed settings ranked by historical tourism potential
item.itunes:title: 5 Assassin's Creed settings ranked by historical tourism potential
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Assassin's Creed exists in modern gaming as massive, open-world games that take their historical settings quite seriously. While you usually have to climb and stab your way through these games, they do function as historical tourism. The franchise has certainly changed over time, and there are some changes we still want to see, but the focus on historically accurate fiction has remained strong. In fact, the more modern games have leaned even further into this, which has resulted in this list being heavily populated by more recent Assassin's Creed games. This list is not based on the quality of the games themselves, although we have ranked the Assassin's Creed games before.

 Related
Hey Ubisoft, can we just get Desmond back in Assassin's Creed?

Desmond Miles deserves more than a footnote. It’s time for Assassin’s Creed to bring him back — and make it matter.

1

5 Assassin’s Creed Unity

While it had a disastrous launch, its French Revolution setting is still incredible

Assassin's Creed Unity is the poster child for bad game launches, coming out in a broken, messy state. The online game didn't work, the single-player didn't work, and it was filled to the brim with bugs. None of that is still true of Assassin's Creed Unity in 2025, though, and underneath all that is a well-made Assassin's Creed in the classic stealth-focused style. On top of that, it features an incredibly detailed France from the late 1700s, during the height of the French Revolution. Not only does this mean an early industrial France, but it means you get to see the country in various states of disaster as the revolution unfolds. It's such a pivotal point in French history, so getting to explore not only the country but one of the biggest moments of its modern history. Sure, the exact events don't involve Assassins and Templars, but Assassin's Creed Unity has plenty of great history for you to explore.

Assassin's Creed Unity
Action
Open-World
Systems
Released
November 11, 2014

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Powered by

4 Assassin's Creed Origins

Let's check out ancient Egypt

Assassin's Creed Origins takes place in 49 B.C. near the end of the Ptolemaic period. This period of time meant that there were sprawling cities and populated areas, but it still firmly feels like what the average person would imagine Ancient Egypt was like. Assassin's Creed Origins covers a large area of Egypt, as Cleopatra attempts to take over the country from her cousin, as Roman soldiers under the command of Julius Ceaser enter the region. Those forces clash multiple times in large-scale battles, but they also impacted the people living in Egypt at the time, which can be seen as you explore the environment.

The first of the open-world modern Assassin's Creed games, Assassin's Creed Origins, doesn't feature the biggest open-world map, but it is still incredibly detailed. Egypt also still feels unique, even among the games set in similar time periods.

Assassin's Creed Origins
Action RPG
Open-World
Systems
Released
October 27, 2017

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Powered by

3 Assassin's Creed Valhalla

A game so big you get to explore a couple of locations

Assassin's Creed Valhalla is the biggest game in the franchise, in terms of length. Because of that massive size, it has several locations, including Norway, England, and Finland. It takes place in 878 A.D., during the great Viking Expansion into Europe. Political pressures cause you to leave Norway and settle in England, which the country of England does not care for. While you can easily spend over 100 hours exploring every inch of these countries in the base game, Assassin's Creed Valhalla also features several DLCs, including ones taking place in Ireland and France, giving you even more historical settings to explore.

The lush green of England and the snowy tundra of Norway make for two great contrasting locations, which is part of what makes Valhalla such an interesting game to play. Even if you don't care about the checklist nature of the modern Assassin's Creed games, this one has too many locales for you to explore to ignore.

Assassin's Creed Valhalla
RPG
Action
Systems
Released
November 10, 2020

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Powered by

2 Assassin's Creed Odyssey

Everybody loves Ancient Greece

Assassin's Creed Odyssey is not only one of the best games in the series, but it also features a setting that is quite popular, Ancient Greece. Set in 341 B.C. during the Peloponnesian War, where Athens and Sparta are battling it out. This game puts you at the center of this conflict, as a former child of Sparta, so not only do you get to explore a large amount of Ancient Greece and the surrounding islands and areas, but you get to meet prominent figures from history. It also features naval combat, so you get to see ships from that era in action.

While Assassin's Creed Odyssey does have several DLCs that are great, they are all based around Ancient Greek myth, taking place in the underworld and other myth-based locations. While those DLCs don't represent actual historical locations, the base game is quite detailed in its depiction of Ancient Greece.

Assassin's Creed Odyssey
Action RPG
Open-World
Systems
Released
October 15, 2018

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Powered by

1 Assassin's Creed Shadows

People were begging for Japan, and Ubisoft delivered

Assassin's Creed Shadows is the most recent game in the franchise, and while the Japanese setting has been done by Ghost of Tsushima, Japan in Shadows is far larger and more detailed. The game takes place in 1597, coming towards the end of Lord Nobunaga's reign in the country, as Westerners start to arrive in the country and different political factions battle it out for control. While in terms of quality, Assassin's Creed Shadows is in the good but not great category, it is the first one exclusive to the Xbox Series X|S and PlayStation 5. That means a massive map, but more importantly, it means a really detailed world.

One of the other next-gen elements is a changing seasons system, where the weather and environment will shift as the seasons pass, which happens at semiregular intervals. While the seasons will change in other games as the story progresses, this constant rotation allows you to experience more parts of the map in different weather and conditions. It cannot be understated how gorgeous Japan looks in Assassin's Creed Shadows. It's worth picking up just for the digital historical tourism.

10 Images
10 Images
Assassin's Creed Shadows
Action
Stealth
RPG
Systems
Released
March 20, 2025

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
7 / 10
Powered by

Oh, the places you'll go

While Assassin's Creed hasn't covered every part of the globe just yet, it has some pretty significant places in history in the franchise. While I didn't mention some of the older games, since the historical tourism aspects have become bigger and more prominent as the series has progressed, there are some incredibly built worlds to explore, like Renaissance-era Italy in Assassin's Creed 2. All the mainline games feature some level of digital tourism, but the more modern games have become true digital vacations.

 Related
5 samurai and shinobi games to play after finishing Assassin’s Creed Shadows

Don't let Assassin's Creed Shadows be the end of your adventures as a samurai and shinobi.

item.title: 5 reasons I love Tailscale Funnels for accessing my self-hosted services
item.itunes:title: 5 reasons I love Tailscale Funnels for accessing my self-hosted services
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
So, I'm a somewhat (some might say very) late convert to Tailscale, and the powerful virtual networking arrangements it facilitates. Some of that is because I've been using various traditional VPN providers, and also because,until recently, I hadn't needed to access my home lab remotely, as I didn't have a home lab to access.

Well, the times be a-changing, and now it's not a question of how I get Tailscale installed, but more of "how many of my devices have I missed off?" See, I might mostly understand networking, DNS, and domains, and how they duct-tape themselves together into what appears as the internet. But that doesn't mean I actually enjoy doing it, such as the complex dance of changing settings in multiple places and ensuring every octet is in the right place.

Don't get me wrong, I love the learning, and the feeling of it working after (sometimes) hours of frustration, but I also have other priorities. My home lab won't run itself, and the more time I spend networking, the less time I can do fun experiments. That's where Tailscale comes in, and more specifically, Tailscale Funnel.

This intrinsic part of Tailscale's MagicDNS means I can test self-hosted services from a public URL without setting up the domain records or anything else network-related I would rather not be doing, so I can focus on the service and getting it working. They're fantastic, and the best part is they don't take long to use.

 Related
5 Tailscale features you didn't know existed

Look, I didn't know about these either.

10

5 They're simple

A one-line command gives me access

I've used a ton of different ways to access my home lab's running services from outside my home network, and most are either VPN solutions that make my device act as if it's at home, reverse proxies to handle passing the data packets through my networking equipment, or a hybrid of the two. Some are complicated to set up, others less so, but they all involve additional steps to get running.

Tailscale Funnel doesn't. Getting Tailscale installed is quick, and setting up a Funnel to expose a self-hosted app is as simple as a single line in a terminal window.

tailscale funnel [port]

That's all it takes to get the service port connected to the external port, an associated DNS record, and a tailnet URL for easy access. It might take a few minutes for DNS records to propagate, but that's as fast as the nameservers can go. No editing YAML files, no syntax or port mapping to remember (or mistype!), just one command that can also use different ports for internal and external if you need them to be that way.

 Related
7 command-line tools that will automate your daily tasks on any OS

Use your time wisely and automate the drudgery of daily tasks.

1

4 They're secure

End-to-end encryption is key

Connecting to my self-hosted apps is easy enough when I'm at home, except for having to deal with self-signed certificates for HTTPS use. However, those self-signed certificates can be an issue when trying to make those apps available outside my house, such as through a properly secured reverse proxy. It's not that it's impossible, but it does require a few more steps to verify the trusted cert, and also some financial outlay.

But as soon as a Tailscale Funnel is initiated, Tailscale gives you a real DNS subdomain, which means you get a certificate provisioned from Tailscale's trust level and therefore automatically trusted by your web browser. It's still a Let's Encrypt self-signed certificate, but you don't have to get your domain, set up a VPS, and point your domain registrar to that to get trusted certificates; it just works. Again, let me reiterate that I hate dealing with the annoyances of DNS, and having something do it all for me seamlessly is near-magical.

When the Funnel URL is used, it sets up a TCP proxy between the associated app and the device on which the URL was clicked. It's fully encrypted and never decrypts the traffic between public devices and your device. The only part of the link that is unencrypted is between the Tailscale server in my home and the app I'm accessing, just as if I was at home.

 Related
4 reasons your cloud provider should be using end-to-end encryption

Using zero knowledge architecture and E2EE makes the web safer for everyone.

3 No need for port forwarding

The fewer open ports from my home lab the better

I'm always wary about leaving ports open to the internet, and so should anyone in this day and age. It's all too easy to set up automated IP scans to identify open ports for future probing, and properly securing them is a significant challenge. If you can port forward in the first place, ISPs often limit this ability, especially on specific ports (port 25 for SMTP is very commonly blocked). That's why I usually don't bother and use something like Pangolin that uses NAT traversal methods to avoid needing any open ports or firewall configuration.

Tailscale Funnels technically do open a port, but only to your tailnet, and not the publicly scannable internet. With an HTTPS-secured URL to access my apps, I don't have to worry about automated attacks or any number of other issues that having a permanently open port on my firewall could pose. I don't even have to worry about changing DNS records. Plus, I don't have to do port forwarding, which I'm glad about every time I set up a Funnel.

 Related
What is port forwarding? Why do I need to do it?

If you want to host applications on your home internet, you'll probably need to port forward. Here's what that means and why.

2 Creative use cases

What if you could make one service, many?

Funnels are powerful as a simple reverse proxy, enabling one self-hosted service to be easily used from a shareable URL. You could use multiple Funnels to access everything in your home lab, but there's a more elegant way that was staring me in the face all this time. The funnel command also supports TCP forwarding, and that means you can set it up to forward to Caddy, or any other locally hosted reverse proxy.

The beauty of this is that the usually annoying to secure part of the reverse proxy never leaves your local network, so there's a significantly reduced attack surface. The only ingress comes through a secure Tailscale URL, so having an authentication provider on your reverse proxy locks out anyone you don't want. It also means you can access apps, services, folders, and other resources that might not be on your tailnet, without having to install the Tailnet client on each one.

 Related
What is internet routing, and why does it matter?

Your internet connection isn't just influenced by things like bandwidth and signal strength, but by routing, too.

1 Easy access for non-Tailscale users

Your family and friends will thank you

So far, every feature I love about Tailscale Funnel has been for personal benefit. But since Funnels are great for sharing your self-hosted services with trusted friends and family members, there's one intrinsic feature that benefits them most. We've all likely experienced pushback when trying to move to self-hosted alternatives, as they can sometimes be more challenging to connect to than the subscriptions they're replacing.

Nobody wants to type in IP addresses or SSH connection details. It's more effort than using SSO to access the service. However, they can, and will, all click on a link (even when we try to prevent them from doing so), making that Tailscale Funnel URL an invaluable part of your strategy. Not everyone wants to learn how things work, but if the barrier to entry is removed, they'll gladly listen to why it's a better fit for their needs.

 Related
I use Tailscale to remotely access my self-hosted services - here's how

Tailscale provides an easy way to access your home server from external networks

9

I might switch to using Tailscale Funnel permanently to save the headaches of DNS and proxies

I've seen the light at the end of the funnel, and Tailscale is about to be one of the first things I install whenever I put a new home lab service or experiment into play. I've always said to focus on what you love in your home lab, and either pay for or outsource the things you don't. And you know what I don't love? Setting up DNS records, port forwards, reverse proxies, and everything else necessary to use self-hosted tools outside my network while staying secure. Tailscale Funnel does all the hard work for me, allowing me to focus on trying new tools and services, which is really what I love about home labbing.

5 They're simple

A one-line command gives me access

I've used a ton of different ways to access my home lab's running services from outside my home network, and most are either VPN solutions that make my device act as if it's at home, reverse proxies to handle passing the data packets through my networking equipment, or a hybrid of the two. Some are complicated to set up, others less so, but they all involve additional steps to get running.

Tailscale Funnel doesn't. Getting Tailscale installed is quick, and setting up a Funnel to expose a self-hosted app is as simple as a single line in a terminal window.

tailscale funnel [port]

That's all it takes to get the service port connected to the external port, an associated DNS record, and a tailnet URL for easy access. It might take a few minutes for DNS records to propagate, but that's as fast as the nameservers can go. No editing YAML files, no syntax or port mapping to remember (or mistype!), just one command that can also use different ports for internal and external if you need them to be that way.

 Related
7 command-line tools that will automate your daily tasks on any OS

Use your time wisely and automate the drudgery of daily tasks.

1

4 They're secure

End-to-end encryption is key

Connecting to my self-hosted apps is easy enough when I'm at home, except for having to deal with self-signed certificates for HTTPS use. However, those self-signed certificates can be an issue when trying to make those apps available outside my house, such as through a properly secured reverse proxy. It's not that it's impossible, but it does require a few more steps to verify the trusted cert, and also some financial outlay.

But as soon as a Tailscale Funnel is initiated, Tailscale gives you a real DNS subdomain, which means you get a certificate provisioned from Tailscale's trust level and therefore automatically trusted by your web browser. It's still a Let's Encrypt self-signed certificate, but you don't have to get your domain, set up a VPS, and point your domain registrar to that to get trusted certificates; it just works. Again, let me reiterate that I hate dealing with the annoyances of DNS, and having something do it all for me seamlessly is near-magical.

When the Funnel URL is used, it sets up a TCP proxy between the associated app and the device on which the URL was clicked. It's fully encrypted and never decrypts the traffic between public devices and your device. The only part of the link that is unencrypted is between the Tailscale server in my home and the app I'm accessing, just as if I was at home.

 Related
4 reasons your cloud provider should be using end-to-end encryption

Using zero knowledge architecture and E2EE makes the web safer for everyone.

3 No need for port forwarding

The fewer open ports from my home lab the better

I'm always wary about leaving ports open to the internet, and so should anyone in this day and age. It's all too easy to set up automated IP scans to identify open ports for future probing, and properly securing them is a significant challenge. If you can port forward in the first place, ISPs often limit this ability, especially on specific ports (port 25 for SMTP is very commonly blocked). That's why I usually don't bother and use something like Pangolin that uses NAT traversal methods to avoid needing any open ports or firewall configuration.

Tailscale Funnels technically do open a port, but only to your tailnet, and not the publicly scannable internet. With an HTTPS-secured URL to access my apps, I don't have to worry about automated attacks or any number of other issues that having a permanently open port on my firewall could pose. I don't even have to worry about changing DNS records. Plus, I don't have to do port forwarding, which I'm glad about every time I set up a Funnel.

 Related
What is port forwarding? Why do I need to do it?

If you want to host applications on your home internet, you'll probably need to port forward. Here's what that means and why.

2 Creative use cases

What if you could make one service, many?

Funnels are powerful as a simple reverse proxy, enabling one self-hosted service to be easily used from a shareable URL. You could use multiple Funnels to access everything in your home lab, but there's a more elegant way that was staring me in the face all this time. The funnel command also supports TCP forwarding, and that means you can set it up to forward to Caddy, or any other locally hosted reverse proxy.

The beauty of this is that the usually annoying to secure part of the reverse proxy never leaves your local network, so there's a significantly reduced attack surface. The only ingress comes through a secure Tailscale URL, so having an authentication provider on your reverse proxy locks out anyone you don't want. It also means you can access apps, services, folders, and other resources that might not be on your tailnet, without having to install the Tailnet client on each one.

 Related
What is internet routing, and why does it matter?

Your internet connection isn't just influenced by things like bandwidth and signal strength, but by routing, too.

1 Easy access for non-Tailscale users

Your family and friends will thank you

So far, every feature I love about Tailscale Funnel has been for personal benefit. But since Funnels are great for sharing your self-hosted services with trusted friends and family members, there's one intrinsic feature that benefits them most. We've all likely experienced pushback when trying to move to self-hosted alternatives, as they can sometimes be more challenging to connect to than the subscriptions they're replacing.

Nobody wants to type in IP addresses or SSH connection details. It's more effort than using SSO to access the service. However, they can, and will, all click on a link (even when we try to prevent them from doing so), making that Tailscale Funnel URL an invaluable part of your strategy. Not everyone wants to learn how things work, but if the barrier to entry is removed, they'll gladly listen to why it's a better fit for their needs.

 Related
I use Tailscale to remotely access my self-hosted services - here's how

Tailscale provides an easy way to access your home server from external networks

9

I might switch to using Tailscale Funnel permanently to save the headaches of DNS and proxies

I've seen the light at the end of the funnel, and Tailscale is about to be one of the first things I install whenever I put a new home lab service or experiment into play. I've always said to focus on what you love in your home lab, and either pay for or outsource the things you don't. And you know what I don't love? Setting up DNS records, port forwards, reverse proxies, and everything else necessary to use self-hosted tools outside my network while staying secure. Tailscale Funnel does all the hard work for me, allowing me to focus on trying new tools and services, which is really what I love about home labbing.
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm a somewhat (some might say very) late convert to Tailscale, and the powerful virtual networking arrangements it facilitates. Some of that is because I've been using various traditional VPN providers, and also because,until recently, I hadn't needed to access my home lab remotely, as I didn't have a home lab to access.

Well, the times be a-changing, and now it's not a question of how I get Tailscale installed, but more of "how many of my devices have I missed off?" See, I might mostly understand networking, DNS, and domains, and how they duct-tape themselves together into what appears as the internet. But that doesn't mean I actually enjoy doing it, such as the complex dance of changing settings in multiple places and ensuring every octet is in the right place.

Don't get me wrong, I love the learning, and the feeling of it working after (sometimes) hours of frustration, but I also have other priorities. My home lab won't run itself, and the more time I spend networking, the less time I can do fun experiments. That's where Tailscale comes in, and more specifically, Tailscale Funnel.

This intrinsic part of Tailscale's MagicDNS means I can test self-hosted services from a public URL without setting up the domain records or anything else network-related I would rather not be doing, so I can focus on the service and getting it working. They're fantastic, and the best part is they don't take long to use.

 Related
5 Tailscale features you didn't know existed

Look, I didn't know about these either.

10

item.title: macOS Tahoe is the last OS update Intel Macs will ever get
item.itunes:title: macOS Tahoe is the last OS update Intel Macs will ever get
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • macOS Tahoe will be the final major software update for Intel-based Macs.
  • Apple confirmed during the WWDC Platforms State of the Union that supported Intel Macs will continue receiving security updates for three more years.
  • While Intel Macs will still get most of macOS Tahoe’s features, capabilities like Apple Intelligence will remain exclusive to Macs powered by Apple Silicon chips.

Apple announced its first Apple Silicon chip, the M1 chip, in November 2020. Between now and then, the Cupertino-based tech giant has continued to expand its silicon lineup with faster, more efficient chips.

Though this does mean better performance and battery life for newer Macs, one thing has always been obvious: Intel Macs were living on borrowed time. Apple announced macOS Tahoe during the WWDC 2025 keynote today, and it's the final major macOS release for Intel-based Macs.

The writing has been on the wall for Intel Macs

Though Apple didn’t mention anything during the WWDC 2025 keynote, Matthew Firlik, Apple’s senior director of developer relations, confirmed during the WWDC Platforms State of the Union that macOS Tahoe will be the final release for Intel Macs.

The 2020 Intel-powered 13-inch MacBook Pro (left) and the 2022 M2-powered 13-inch MacBook Pro (right).

After going over the benefits of Apple Silicon and how the company has managed to add “incredible features” like Apple Intelligence and Game Mode since beginning the transition from Intel to Apple Silicon, Apple urged developers to help users migrate to the Apple Silicon versions of their apps.

Though they didn’t expand much on it, it’s been confirmed that Intel-based Macs still supported in macOS Tahoe will continue to receive security updates for three more years. This includes the following models:

  • MacBook Pro (16-inch, 2019)
  • MacBook Pro (13-inch, 2020, Four Thunderbolt 3 ports)
  • iMac (27-inch, 2020)
  • Mac Pro (2019)

You’ll still get a lot of the exciting upgrades coming with macOS Tahoe, like the design refresh with the new Liquid Glass material, the Continuity features, Spotlight upgrades, and essentially all features, excluding the ones that rely on Apple Intelligence. This is because Apple Intelligence only works on Macs equipped with Apple Silicon chips.

When Apple first announced the transition to Apple Silicon, it mentioned that it would continue to “support and release new versions of macOS for Intel-based Macs for years to come.” Though the company has been slowly dropping software support for Intel-based Macs, it’s now official that the last models still hanging on will get their final major update with macOS Tahoe.

So if you’ve been holding onto your Intel-based Mac and aren’t ready to bid farewell to it just yet, it might be time. Apple’s said its goodbyes, so now might be a good time to consider finally upgrading to one with an M-chip.

item.title: 5 PC hardware and peripheral trends that are slowly dying
item.itunes:title: 5 PC hardware and peripheral trends that are slowly dying
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The world of PCs is evolving rapidly. Because of this rapid evolution, we constantly welcome many new PC trends and say goodbye to many old trends that have engaged enthusiasts for years. For better or worse, these old trends slowly faded away, but that doesn’t mean we can’t remember fondly.

One important note here is that we’ll only be discussing ‌the mainstream PC trends, not anything about legacy or other segments of the computer world, like NAS, server, or SBC trends.

5 Using SATA storage drives

Maybe not going into Silicon Heaven soon

SATA is still a widely used storage connection protocol for hard drives and SSDs. But things have started to move on slowly. Many pre-built systems today are only offered with an NVME SSD as a storage option. Most PC builders today only build PCs with NVME SSDs. Plenty of PC builders prefer NVME SSDs only, especially when the budget is more than one grand. So, it’s clear that SATA will eventually meet the same fate as its predecessor, PATA.

It won't happen soon because NAS drives are still one of the most popular uses of the SATA standard. Some legacy servers still use SATA storage drives for their everyday operation. But one day, we have to say goodbye to SATA from the scene of PC building. Maybe SATA will not become extinct as soon as next year, but eventually will find its way to silicon heaven after 10 to 12 years, and that time is shorter if mainstream PCs are the only consideration.

 Related
How to choose an SSD: What is NAND, TLC, PCIe, and SATA?

Find out everything there is to know about your SSD.

4 Low-profile GPUs

Almost extinct nowadays

Witnessing the slow death of low-profile GPUs is painful for any PC-building enthusiast. The last proper low-profile cooler-ready GPU was the Nvidia GeForce RTX 3050 6 GB edition. There are exceptional custom and more powerful GPU models, like the GeForce RTX 4060 low-profile edition from Gigabyte. However, the chip makers are not trying much to create a GPU suitable for low-profile coolers.

That doesn’t mean the low-profile PC-building community is dead; low-profile GPUs still find their use cases in many situations. There are dedicated people who are trying numerous mods to fit high-end GPUs into low-profile cases. Casual buyers who want a low-profile build usually purchase a GTX 1650 LP.

Many builders recommend pairing the newer RTX 3050 6 GB with a cheap OptiPlex build for a budget gaming machine. But this trend of using low-profile GPUs is simply vanishing. Unless GPU manufacturers focus more on this market, there is simply no hope for the revival of this trend.

 Related
4 reasons why low-profile GPUs are great for home labs and NAS

Power-hungry flagship graphics cards may be great for gaming PCs, but you'll want something lighter for your home server

3 Keyboard with a numpad

It’s considered old school now

Ask 10 PC owners if they use a keyboard with a numpad, and most would likely answer a straight no. The answer isn’t surprising, considering many PC users nowadays don't even need a numpad for daily driving, especially those playing Esports titles. The industry itself is shifting in that direction. Fully customizable full-sized keyboards are getting less and less common in the scene, and many enthusiasts prefer a numpad-less keyboard over a traditional “old school” full-sized keyboard with a numpad.

Of course, every keyboard doesn’t have to be full-sized to fit a numpad on it. Many smaller form factors have a numpad (e.g., 96% keyboards), but they aren’t even considered “popular” compared to the hype for the TKL form factor. So, maybe keyboards with a numpad won't be extinct anytime soon, but considering the trend of the industry, it’s obvious that we can easily put this meta on the list of slowly vanishing metas.

2 High-end GPUs with efficient power draw requirements

It’s an oxymoron at this point

When did you last witness a high-end GPU that didn’t draw a significant amount of your power budget? Of course, a GPU will be power-hungry if it’s powerful enough, but I’m talking about efficiency, not low power requirements. There has been a trend of increasing power requirements in the last three generations, which has been increasing significantly. The tables below will give a clear picture of the point.

GPU model (80 Ti/Super class) GPU TDP
Nvidia GeForce GTX 980 Ti 250W
Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080 Ti 250W (+0%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 2080 Ti 250W (+0%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 3080 Ti 350W (+ 40%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 4080 Super 320W (-9.14%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5080 360W (+12.5%)

From this table, we can see that the 80 Ti/Super series doesn’t increase its power requirements at the scale of the 90" Titan series. But it’s still noticeable. In particular, the 30 series GPUs witnessed a significant jump in their TDP requirements. The 3080 Ti has a whopping TDP of 350W, whereas the 2080 Ti only has a TDP of 250W. However, we see the breakpoint of this trend with the release of the RTX 4080 Super. In short, you have to get a chunky PSU nowadays, unlike previous days when you could literally use the same PSU for the 980 Ti and the 1080 Ti.

GPU model (90/Titan class) GPU TDP
Nvidia Titan XP 250W
Nvidia Titan RTX 280W (+12%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 3090 350W (+25%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 4090 450W (+28.5%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5090 575W (+27.78%)

Things were okay when Nvidia didn’t introduce the 90-class lineup. However, things got a lot worse when Nvidia released the GeForce RTX 4090; with its humongous size and power requirements, many PC builders were not happy. The RTX 4090 was still justifiable for its decent performance uplift. That's not the case for the GeForce RTX 5090, unfortunately. It barely reflects any efficiency gains of the Blackwell architecture on which it’s based. So, in short, we may see this trend continue unless any of the manufacturers achieve a huge architectural milestone.

1 Cheap entry-level GPUs

Getting replaced by APUs

Almost every PC builder misses those cheap entry-level GPUs that could at least handle the latest games at medium or high settings while also being realistically budget-friendly. Nowadays, we barely see a GPU that costs under 200. The last affordable GPU release was by Intel, and it was their ARC B580 with an MSRP of only 250 USD. However, that MSRP wasn’t maintained when it launched on the shelves; you cannot find the B580 for a good price, even at the time of writing this article. And GPUs for absolutely entry-level systems are non-existent nowadays.

Sadly, affordable GPUs aren’t returning anytime soon. As integrated graphics are getting stronger than ever, manufacturers will focus less and less on the affordable GPU market over time. And one day, APUs will eventually replace the entry-level GPUs.

At the time of writing, the most powerful desktop APU, the Ryzen 7 8700G, is comparable to a popular entry-level GPU, the GeForce GTX 1650. But people who use an older CPU plus don't want to upgrade to a newer platform are in real trouble with the decline of these ultra-budget GPUs. So, the rise of APUs is not a clear win yet, but things may change in the future with the rapid development of APUs. Sadly, users who are still stuck with older platforms may not benefit from this development.

 Related
3 reasons I really want Intel to release another Battlemage GPU

We all need the Intel Battlemage train to keep going

1

This is the center of dogma of the technology world

It’s nothing new to say goodbye to many trends every so often, as the world of technology never stops for anything. Some trends will be missed, some will not be. And the trends mentioned above will one day find their way to silicon heaven. Still, their trace could be found in the minds of the old PC builders.

 Related
How to choose an SSD: What is NAND, TLC, PCIe, and SATA?

Find out everything there is to know about your SSD.

4 Low-profile GPUs

Almost extinct nowadays

Witnessing the slow death of low-profile GPUs is painful for any PC-building enthusiast. The last proper low-profile cooler-ready GPU was the Nvidia GeForce RTX 3050 6 GB edition. There are exceptional custom and more powerful GPU models, like the GeForce RTX 4060 low-profile edition from Gigabyte. However, the chip makers are not trying much to create a GPU suitable for low-profile coolers.

That doesn’t mean the low-profile PC-building community is dead; low-profile GPUs still find their use cases in many situations. There are dedicated people who are trying numerous mods to fit high-end GPUs into low-profile cases. Casual buyers who want a low-profile build usually purchase a GTX 1650 LP.

Many builders recommend pairing the newer RTX 3050 6 GB with a cheap OptiPlex build for a budget gaming machine. But this trend of using low-profile GPUs is simply vanishing. Unless GPU manufacturers focus more on this market, there is simply no hope for the revival of this trend.

 Related
4 reasons why low-profile GPUs are great for home labs and NAS

Power-hungry flagship graphics cards may be great for gaming PCs, but you'll want something lighter for your home server

3 Keyboard with a numpad

It’s considered old school now

Ask 10 PC owners if they use a keyboard with a numpad, and most would likely answer a straight no. The answer isn’t surprising, considering many PC users nowadays don't even need a numpad for daily driving, especially those playing Esports titles. The industry itself is shifting in that direction. Fully customizable full-sized keyboards are getting less and less common in the scene, and many enthusiasts prefer a numpad-less keyboard over a traditional “old school” full-sized keyboard with a numpad.

Of course, every keyboard doesn’t have to be full-sized to fit a numpad on it. Many smaller form factors have a numpad (e.g., 96% keyboards), but they aren’t even considered “popular” compared to the hype for the TKL form factor. So, maybe keyboards with a numpad won't be extinct anytime soon, but considering the trend of the industry, it’s obvious that we can easily put this meta on the list of slowly vanishing metas.

2 High-end GPUs with efficient power draw requirements

It’s an oxymoron at this point

When did you last witness a high-end GPU that didn’t draw a significant amount of your power budget? Of course, a GPU will be power-hungry if it’s powerful enough, but I’m talking about efficiency, not low power requirements. There has been a trend of increasing power requirements in the last three generations, which has been increasing significantly. The tables below will give a clear picture of the point.

GPU model (80 Ti/Super class) GPU TDP
Nvidia GeForce GTX 980 Ti 250W
Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080 Ti 250W (+0%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 2080 Ti 250W (+0%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 3080 Ti 350W (+ 40%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 4080 Super 320W (-9.14%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5080 360W (+12.5%)

From this table, we can see that the 80 Ti/Super series doesn’t increase its power requirements at the scale of the 90" Titan series. But it’s still noticeable. In particular, the 30 series GPUs witnessed a significant jump in their TDP requirements. The 3080 Ti has a whopping TDP of 350W, whereas the 2080 Ti only has a TDP of 250W. However, we see the breakpoint of this trend with the release of the RTX 4080 Super. In short, you have to get a chunky PSU nowadays, unlike previous days when you could literally use the same PSU for the 980 Ti and the 1080 Ti.

GPU model (90/Titan class) GPU TDP
Nvidia Titan XP 250W
Nvidia Titan RTX 280W (+12%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 3090 350W (+25%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 4090 450W (+28.5%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5090 575W (+27.78%)

Things were okay when Nvidia didn’t introduce the 90-class lineup. However, things got a lot worse when Nvidia released the GeForce RTX 4090; with its humongous size and power requirements, many PC builders were not happy. The RTX 4090 was still justifiable for its decent performance uplift. That's not the case for the GeForce RTX 5090, unfortunately. It barely reflects any efficiency gains of the Blackwell architecture on which it’s based. So, in short, we may see this trend continue unless any of the manufacturers achieve a huge architectural milestone.

1 Cheap entry-level GPUs

Getting replaced by APUs

Almost every PC builder misses those cheap entry-level GPUs that could at least handle the latest games at medium or high settings while also being realistically budget-friendly. Nowadays, we barely see a GPU that costs under 200. The last affordable GPU release was by Intel, and it was their ARC B580 with an MSRP of only 250 USD. However, that MSRP wasn’t maintained when it launched on the shelves; you cannot find the B580 for a good price, even at the time of writing this article. And GPUs for absolutely entry-level systems are non-existent nowadays.

Sadly, affordable GPUs aren’t returning anytime soon. As integrated graphics are getting stronger than ever, manufacturers will focus less and less on the affordable GPU market over time. And one day, APUs will eventually replace the entry-level GPUs.

At the time of writing, the most powerful desktop APU, the Ryzen 7 8700G, is comparable to a popular entry-level GPU, the GeForce GTX 1650. But people who use an older CPU plus don't want to upgrade to a newer platform are in real trouble with the decline of these ultra-budget GPUs. So, the rise of APUs is not a clear win yet, but things may change in the future with the rapid development of APUs. Sadly, users who are still stuck with older platforms may not benefit from this development.

 Related
3 reasons I really want Intel to release another Battlemage GPU

We all need the Intel Battlemage train to keep going

1

This is the center of dogma of the technology world

It’s nothing new to say goodbye to many trends every so often, as the world of technology never stops for anything. Some trends will be missed, some will not be. And the trends mentioned above will one day find their way to silicon heaven. Still, their trace could be found in the minds of the old PC builders.
item.content:encoded:
The world of PCs is evolving rapidly. Because of this rapid evolution, we constantly welcome many new PC trends and say goodbye to many old trends that have engaged enthusiasts for years. For better or worse, these old trends slowly faded away, but that doesn’t mean we can’t remember fondly.

One important note here is that we’ll only be discussing ‌the mainstream PC trends, not anything about legacy or other segments of the computer world, like NAS, server, or SBC trends.

5 Using SATA storage drives

Maybe not going into Silicon Heaven soon

SATA is still a widely used storage connection protocol for hard drives and SSDs. But things have started to move on slowly. Many pre-built systems today are only offered with an NVME SSD as a storage option. Most PC builders today only build PCs with NVME SSDs. Plenty of PC builders prefer NVME SSDs only, especially when the budget is more than one grand. So, it’s clear that SATA will eventually meet the same fate as its predecessor, PATA.

It won't happen soon because NAS drives are still one of the most popular uses of the SATA standard. Some legacy servers still use SATA storage drives for their everyday operation. But one day, we have to say goodbye to SATA from the scene of PC building. Maybe SATA will not become extinct as soon as next year, but eventually will find its way to silicon heaven after 10 to 12 years, and that time is shorter if mainstream PCs are the only consideration.

 Related
How to choose an SSD: What is NAND, TLC, PCIe, and SATA?

Find out everything there is to know about your SSD.

4 Low-profile GPUs

 Related
4 reasons why low-profile GPUs are great for home labs and NAS

Power-hungry flagship graphics cards may be great for gaming PCs, but you'll want something lighter for your home server

3 Keyboard with a numpad

It’s considered old school now

Of course, every keyboard doesn’t have to be full-sized to fit a numpad on it. Many smaller form factors have a numpad (e.g., 96% keyboards), but they aren’t even considered “popular” compared to the hype for the TKL form factor. So, maybe keyboards with a numpad won't be extinct anytime soon, but considering the trend of the industry, it’s obvious that we can easily put this meta on the list of slowly vanishing metas.

Nvidia GeForce RTX 2080 Ti 250W (+0%)

Nvidia GeForce RTX 4080 Super 320W (-9.14%)
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5080 360W (+12.5%)

item.title: HP's versatile 2-in-1 laptop with AMD's Ryzen AI 7 and 24GB RAM just dropped $350
item.itunes:title: HP's versatile 2-in-1 laptop with AMD's Ryzen AI 7 and 24GB RAM just dropped $350
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
HP OmniBook X Flip
$750 $1100 Save $350

A 16-inch laptop with plenty of power under the hood. Get it now for its lowest price yet, with a steep discount that knocks $350 off.

$750 at Best Buy

If you're looking for a versatile laptop that's sleek and has lots of power, this one from HP is going to be right up your alley. The HP Omnibook X Flip is a convertible laptop that features an AMD Ryzen AI 7 processor that's paired with 24GB of RAM and 1TB of SSD storage.

Related
Best laptops in 2025

Looking for a new laptop for work, school, or anything else? Here are the very best laptops on the market right now.

This is a laptop that can handle pretty much anything you can throw at it, and while it typically comes in at a whopping $1,100, it can now be had for much less, thanks to a stellar discount from Best Buy that drops it down to just $750. This is the lowest price we've seen for this model, making it a fantastic time to pick one up.

What's great about the HP Omnibook X Flip?

In addition to having a pretty powerful set of specifications, this laptop also features a large 16-inch 2K touchscreen. Although having a lot of screen real estate can be great for work, it's also perfect if you're looking to make this your main entertainment device.

While this laptop isn't a dedicated gaming machine, it's still plenty capable when it comes to games, thanks to its AMD Radeon 860M graphics card. Although you won't get top-end frame rates, you will still be able to play AAA titles without any issues. You also get support for AMD FreeSync technology, which will help the gameplay look more fluid.

While the laptop is a bit on the heavier side, coming in at 4.18 pounds, that's to be expected with such a large screen. On the other hand, the laptop is quite thin, measuring in at 0.61 inches, but also delivers impressive battery life, with HP sharing that the OmniBook should last up to 21 hours.

Connectivity is also quite good as well, with Wi-Fi 6E, Bluetooth 5.3, two USB-C, two USB-A, DisplayPort, HDMI, and a 3.5mm audio jack. For the most part, you're getting a lot of laptop here for your money, making this one of those laptops that really does deliver a lot of value. Just be sure to get it while it's still on sale because a deal like this won't last long.

item.title: I paired NotebookLM with Perplexity for a week, and it feels like they’re meant to work together
item.itunes:title: I paired NotebookLM with Perplexity for a week, and it feels like they’re meant to work together
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I've been using Google's AI-powered personalized research assistant, NotebookLM, since it was launched as an experimental product, and I've covered it a bunch recently. And honestly? I'm still not over it, and it's easily my favorite AI productivity tool.

I've always used NotebookLM independently. Despite seeing people experiment and pair NotebookLM with tools like OneNote, I never felt the need to team it up with anything else, since what it offered felt complete on its own.

But in the past week, I decided to finally switch things up, and brought another AI tool I've been playing around with for a while now into the mix: Perplexity. My intention here wasn't to find a NotebookLM replacement. Instead, I wanted to see what would happen if I used both of them together.

Would I go back to using NotebookLM on its own like I have for months? Or would it completely transform my workflow, for the better? To my surprise, it was the latter, and the two AI tools complement each way better than I expected.

 Related
8 strategies for getting the most out of Perplexity

Don't let it perplex you

Why I paired NotebookLM with Perplexity in the first place

They pick up where each other leaves off

Though NotebookLM and Perplexity are both designed to make the tedious research process much quicker and simpler, they tackle completely different parts of the research process. Perplexity is an AI search engine, and is designed to help cut down on the amount of time you spend searching for information.

On the other hand, NotebookLM's work comes in after you've gathered your information. It can help you organize, understand and make sense of the topic you're researching and engage with it in different ways. Though Google added a Discover sources feature to NotebookLM not too long ago, which allows you to describe what kind of sources they'd like to add to their notebook and can then search the web for you, it has quite a few limitations.

For starters, it can only curate a maximum of ten recommended sources at a time. I've also not always been satisfied with the sources it gathers for me, so I typically stick to finding sources from the web myself. Perplexity, on the other hand, is designed to search the internet in real-time and gather information from authoritative sources.

So, essentially, where NotebookLM lacks, Perplexity shines (and vice versa). And when you merge the strengths of both the AI tools, you get a research combo that's smarter and way more effective than using either alone.

 Related
NotebookLM is already great, but these 4 features would make it even better

Good? Yes. Perfect? Not yet.

5

Perplexity handled finding the sources

It does all the heavy lifting

As I mentioned above, the key here was to leverage the strengths of NotebookLM and Perplexity to their fullest. And the way I used the tools was ridiculously simple. Perplexity hunts down sources relevant to the topic I'm studying, I create a new NotebookLM notebook and upload all the sources, and then use NotebookLM’s various features to engage with the information. That likely sounds a bit confusing, so I’ll illustrate it with an example.

Given that I'm a computer science major, say I'm studying for an upcoming midterm for my Object-Oriented Programming course, and I'm confused about the key principles of the paradigm. Though one option is to upload my lecture slides directly to NotebookLM, let's say I want to see how the web explains it instead and that my lecture slides don't have sufficient information.

Instead of manually scouring the web, what I'll do instead is ask Perplexity to find reliable sources that focus on the topic at hand instead. Here's an example prompt:

Find credible sources explaining the key principles of object-oriented programming, including encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism. Provide links to articles, tutorials, or papers that offer clear examples and explanations

Within seconds, Perplexity compiled the insights and answered the query I had (i.e., explained the key principles of Object-Oriented Programming). That’s not really what I’m using Perplexity for here, though. Instead, there’s a Sources tab next to the Answer tab which, as you may have figured, includes a list of all the sources it pulled information from.

NotebookLM helps organize and engage with sources Perplexity finds

It organizes the chaos Perplexity finds

Once Perplexity hunts down the sources I need, I create a new notebook in NotebookLM and upload all of them. Here's where it gets fun. Once the sources are uploaded, I can use NotebookLM to generate an Audio Overview if I'm not in the mood to read through them and would rather listen to a podcast-style discussion about the sources.

Given that I'm not fully clear on the different concepts in the sources, I'll likely have a few questions. In that case, I can use the interactive mode in Audio Overviews instead and hop right into the discussion the two AI hosts are having to clear my doubts.

If I want to see how different concepts in the sources connect together, I can generate a Mind Map instead. NotebookLM can also create a Study Guide, Briefing doc, FAQ, or Timeline using my sources.

The best part is that NotebookLM only references information from the sources you feed it. This means I can ask it questions in the Chat panel without worrying too much about hallucinations or it pulling answers from random places online.

In comparison, asking a question about a source in Perplexity can be a bit of a chore. Instead of answering based on the first set of sources it found for you, it typically fetches a fresh batch and uses those to respond, so it doesn’t always stick to the materials you originally wanted to focus on.

Though this example likely won’t have fresh new information a couple of days after, if you’re studying something more real-time that’s constantly evolving, Perplexity can really come in clutch. After compiling your original sources and using them, you can use Perplexity again later to get updated sources with the latest information since it searches the web in real time.

 Related
Until NotebookLM, I never believed AI could be this game-changing for productivity

It transformed my view of AI, for the better.

7

NotebookLM and Perplexity are already powerful, but together they’re unstoppable

Despite the two AI tools serving different purposes, they work seamlessly together. Even though I’ve used Perplexity quite a bit in the past, I never really thought about pairing it with NotebookLM until now. And I’m genuinely glad I did — better late than never, right?
